claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me start by asking about your child's age, as this is the first step in determining eligibility.

**How old is your child?**

The age categories that matter for Child Benefit are:
- **Under 16**: Generally automatically qualify
- **16-19**: May qualify if in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Generally not eligible

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children you'd like to ask about, please let me know all their ages.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - Alex, who is 19 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 19 years old.

Since Alex is between 16 and 19 years old, eligibility for Child Benefit depends on whether they are in approved education or training. Let me navigate to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to understand Alex's current education or training status. 

**Is Alex currently:**
1. **In approved education or training** (enrolled and attending courses)
2. **Recently left education** (within the last 20 weeks)
3. **Neither** (not in education/training and left more than 20 weeks ago)

Which situation applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is currently in education - he's studying for his A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is currently in education studying for A-levels. Let me check the next step to verify the education level requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! A-levels are an approved (non-advanced) qualification for Child Benefit purposes. Now I need to check a few more details about Alex's education:

**Is Alex's A-level course:**
1. **Full-time** (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)?
2. **Not funded by an employer** or part of a work agreement for a future job?

Most standard A-level courses at school or college are full-time and meet these requirements. Can you confirm this is the case for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - he's doing full-time A-levels at sixth form college, nothing to do with any employer.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex's A-level course at sixth form college qualifies as approved education. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check Alex's work status:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

For Child Benefit purposes, if a 16-19 year old works 24 hours or more per week, they're not considered a qualifying child, even if they're in education. Working fewer than 24 hours per week (or not working at all) is fine.

What is Alex's current work situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Alex does work full-time - he has a job at the local supermarket alongside his studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I need to clarify this important detail. You mentioned Alex works "full-time" - can you tell me **how many hours per week** Alex works at the supermarket?

The reason this matters is that for Child Benefit purposes:
- Working **less than 24 hours per week** is fine, even alongside studies
- Working **24 hours or more per week** would make Alex ineligible for Child Benefit

So if Alex is working, say, 20 hours per week, that would still be okay. But if it's 24 hours or more per week, that would affect eligibility.

How many hours per week does Alex typically work?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's actually 24 hours or more per week - he works quite a bit alongside his college work.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that. This is an important factor. Let me navigate to the outcome based on Alex working 24 hours or more per week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

**Here's why:**

Although Alex is 19 years old and studying for A-levels (which is approved education), the fact that he is working **24 or more hours per week** means he doesn't qualify as a "qualifying child" for Child Benefit purposes. The rules state that children aged 16 or over who work 24+ hours per week are not eligible, even if they're in approved education.

**What this means:**

- If Alex was working fewer than 24 hours per week, he would qualify for Child Benefit while studying for his A-levels
- Child Benefit stops when a child aged 16+ starts working 24 or more hours per week

**Possible future eligibility:**

If Alex's work situation changes in the future (for example, if he reduces his hours to under 24 per week or stops working to focus on his studies), you may be able to claim Child Benefit again, as long as he remains in approved education or training.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit eligibility?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I see, that's disappointing but thank you for explaining it clearly. I understand now - it's because he's working too many hours even though he's still studying. No, I don't have any other questions. Thank you for your help.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
You're very welcome, and I'm glad I could explain it clearly for you. I know it's disappointing, especially since Alex is still in full-time education, but the 24-hour work threshold is a firm rule.

Just remember - if Alex's circumstances change (reduces work hours below 24 per week, or stops working to focus on studies), you could become eligible again while he's still in approved education or training.

Best of luck to Alex with his A-levels! If you ever need to check eligibility again in the future, feel free to come back. Take care!

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and studying full-time A-levels at sixth form college, which is approved non-advanced education. However, Alex works 24 or more hours per week at a local supermarket. Under Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24+ hours per week do not qualify as a 'qualifying child' for Child Benefit purposes, even if they are in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (19 years old), who is in approved education (A-levels) but works 24 or more hours per week. Due to the work hours exceeding the 24-hour weekly threshold, Alex does not qualify as a qualifying child under Child Benefit rules, making the claimant ineligible for Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.reason: Child aged 16+ is working 24 or more hours per week and not in approved education or training
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops if child aged 16 or over starts paid work for 24+ hours per week and is no longer in approved education or training. If they return to education/training, you may be able to claim again.

Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
